title: Distance, Dot Products, and Cosine Similarity
learningObjectives: Understand distance, dot products, and cosine similarity
Recognize when to use each comparison method

whyThisLesson.text: Measuring similarity between vectors is critical in analyzing representations. We need ways to tell if two activations represent the same concept.

intuition.analogy: Distance is how far apart two things are physically. Cosine similarity is whether they are pointing in the same direction, regardless of how far away they are.
intuition.analogyLimit: In high dimensions, nearly all random vectors are orthogonal (cosine similarity ~0).
intuition.text: We introduce Euclidean distance, dot product, and cosine similarity to compare vectors. b=2a so cos=1 despite being different vectors. Cosine IGNORES magnitude.

toyExample.description: Three comparison methods for vectors a=[3,4], b=[6,8], c=[4,-3].

steps.insight: Euclidean distance measures magnitude of difference.

steps.action: Compute Dot product a·b and a·c
steps.insight: Dot product combines magnitude and direction.

visual.question: How do distance and angle differ?
visual.description: Showing vectors of different lengths with the same angle, and vectors of same length with different angles.

technical.text: Distance is the L2 norm of difference. Dot product is sum of element-wise products. Cosine similarity is dot product normalized by magnitudes.

mathematics.label: Euclidean distance
mathematics.plainExplanation: Straight line distance between a and b.

evidence.label: [Cosine properties]
evidence.paraphrase: Cosine similarity is 1.0 for vectors pointing in the exact same direction, regardless of length.

failureModes.name: Using cosine when magnitude matters
failureModes.symptom: Treating low-confidence and high-confidence activations as identical.
failureModes.mitigation: Use dot product or distance instead.

failureModes.name: Using Euclidean when only direction matters
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Check if vectors point the same way.

setconcaConnection.relevance: SetConCA needs to measure how similar features are.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Why might cosine similarity be misleading?
masteryQuestions.goodVersion: It ignores magnitude, which might encode feature strength or confidence.

bridgeToNext.text: Next we will look at statistics over collections of vectors.
